name: IceCube astrophysical neutrino source origin
summary: IceCube has observed a diffuse flux of astrophysical neutrinos from TeV to PeV energies (E^2 phi ~ 10^-8 GeV/cm^2/s/sr), but only two point sources are confirmed: blazar TXS 0506+056 (2018, Science 361, 147 - associated with a flaring blazar) and Seyfert NGC 1068 (2022, Science 378, 538 - 4.2 sigma). The bulk of the diffuse flux remains unattributed - the expected populations of starburst galaxies, AGN, and cosmic-ray-reservoir galaxy clusters individually under-account, and no anisotropy correlates with any cataloged source class. Either the cosmic-ray acceleration physics produces neutrinos more efficiently than gamma-rays (the 'multi-messenger gap'), or there exists an unexpected neutrino emitter population.

characteristic_numbers.note: Observed flux at ~100 TeV (per-flavor); fit by single power-law with spectral index ~2.5. IceCube 9.5-yr HESE arXiv:2011.03545.
Adding all known/expected blazar + starburst + AGN contributions accounts for at most ~30% of the observed diffuse flux. Origin of the rest unidentified.

principles_stressed.note: Beyond-SM interpretations include heavy dark-matter decay (m_DM ~ TeV-PeV, tau ~ 10^28 s), Lorentz-invariance violation at the Planck scale modifying neutrino dispersion, and sterile-neutrino oscillation-induced spectral features. Standard-astrophysics explanations (e.g., 'hidden' cosmic-ray accelerators) are simpler but require missing physics about source environments.

history: First astrophysical-flux evidence: IceCube 2013, Science 342, 1242856 (HESE 2-yr). PeV cascade events 'Bert' and 'Ernie' 2012. Glashow resonance event 2021 (Nature 591, 220). TXS 0506+056 multimessenger campaign 2018 (Science 361, 147 and 361, 146). NGC 1068 evidence 2022 (Science 378, 538). Galactic-plane neutrino emission detected 2023 (Science 380, 1338).

references.citation: IceCube Collaboration, 'Neutrino emission from the direction of the blazar TXS 0506+056 prior to the IceCube-170922A alert', Science 361, 147 (2018)
IceCube Collaboration, 'Evidence for neutrino emission from the nearby active galaxy NGC 1068', Science 378, 538 (2022)
